topic: An adult child moves back in with their parents
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the house was a heavy, suffocating blanket. I kept my shoulders hunched, tracing patterns on the worn fabric of the armchair with my thumb. Every shared glance felt like an interrogation, every casual comment about my life a subtle jab. I wanted to bolt, to find a place where the air didn't feel thick with unspoken expectations. When Mom started reorganizing the pantry again, her movements were sharp and overly precise, and I found myself physically tensing, my jaw clenching so tightly it ached. "I've got this, honey," she chirped, not looking up, the sound grating on my nerves like sandpaper. I just nodded, a tight, shallow movement, and retreated to the guest room, pulling the door shut with a decisive, almost violent click.

[story 2]

I spent the entire afternoon meticulously cleaning the kitchen, scrubbing the same spot on the countertop until my knuckles were white. The water ran cold over my hands, and I kept catching my reflection in the polished steel, seeing only a stranger—tired, brittle. Dad walked in, humming tunelessly, and the sound immediately made my stomach twist. "You don't have to do everything, sweetheart," he said, his tone laced with a weary sort of resignation that stung more than any accusation. I stopped scrubbing mid-motion, the sponge slipping from my grasp. I stared at the soapy residue clinging to my fingers, a sudden, hot wave of wanting to scream washing over me, but I swallowed it down, forcing a neutral expression onto my face.

[story 3]

The evenings stretched out like endless, unyielding hallways. I found myself pacing the perimeter of the living room, my feet making soft, agitated taps on the hardwood floor. Every time my mother asked about my job prospects, a tightness seized my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I started answering with clipped, one-word responses, shutting down the conversation before it could spiral into the tedious details I couldn't bear to relive. Later, while trying to read, I kept shifting in my seat, the fabric of the sofa digging into my spine. I wished for an escape route, a door I could slam and never look back through, but the weight of the shared space kept anchoring me in place.
